## [06:03 - 09:05]

And then they have some partners to pay. And so their eight hundred and fifty thousand dollar launch all of a sudden becomes a four hundred and fifty thousand dollar launch. First of all, there you want to think something that's easy to negotiate ahead of time, but hard after is you want to get access to the shopping cart. You want to have the login details so you can actually watch the orders coming in so you can verify what the numbers are for the launch. You don't want to just depend on them telling you what the numbers are because most people are honest, but not everyone's honest, right? So when you can actually look in the cart and say, this is the exact number, then it's clear for everyone exactly what the sales were and exactly what you should get paid. Another way you could do this, this is a little harder, but I've seen this done. The money actually flows through you. If you've got a merchant account, the orders come into your account and then you take your percentage and then you pay them. Now most deals are not done this way, but I have seen it done. If you look at it, the book world does it this way. You see in the book world, we're talking hardcover books, not hardcover, but real physical books, real publishers that have been doing this for centuries, the money goes through them, the royalties go through them, they take out their piece and then they send you the check.

## [09:05 - 12:12]

So that's something to consider. One thing is, I have seen million dollar launches where they do the million dollar launch and all of a sudden there's no money left over. Somehow, all the money disappears. So you want to negotiate when you get paid, when you get paid. Hopefully it's within, it's X percent of it within a week or two and the balance within 30 days or something along that lines, but be very clear on when the money gets paid. When I talk about this, I mean all the money, the upfront money, the retainer money, but especially the percentage of the launch money. One thing you want to negotiate is relaunches. Like if you go ahead and put together this launch, the beautiful thing about it is most of the hard work is done and generally you can do a relaunch six months or nine months or a year later and there's almost no extra work for either you or your partner. But one thing is, if you're going to just do all that work upfront and then they're able to do the relaunch without compensating on that, that's fine because every deal is a deal, but then you should probably get a bigger chunk upfront. I think a better way to do it is you get paid on the initial launch and you also get paid on the relaunch. Maybe on the relaunch you get a lower percentage. I don't know. Every deal is a deal. You can negotiate anything you want, but do not do the deal without at least thinking about the relaunch and how you get paid on the relaunch or whether you get paid on the relaunch. It's a huge item to negotiate and a lot of people never even think about the relaunch, but the reality is you're putting up so much work upfront and that work is going to be reusable. So think about the relaunch. I've already talked about the different types of fees, the retainer, the upfront, the percentage of the launch. Another thing that could be on the table is equity, actually having an ownership piece of the business. Having an ownership piece of the list that is generated is another option. Getting a mailing. Maybe say you're helping someone and it's your neighbor and the neighbor is going to do a product about training horses. Sometimes maybe you have a business about training horses as well. I've seen this type of thing happen a lot where someone who's already in the market helps someone else. Maybe you can negotiate that you'll have a launch list when you come out of that launch and that launch list is going to be a red hot super warm list. Maybe you negotiate a mailing or two mailings or three mailings into your business, into your product, into your next launch, something to put on the table and to think about. So final, final, final. You deal as a deal and it's much, much easier to negotiate upfront and you want to get all these things as crystal clear as possible. ## [12:12 - 13:39]

that's readable by regular humans, not by lawyers. And also another great thing to put in there is how is your relationship going to end? What does the end of your relationship look like? What's the out clause for each of you? This is something that can protect both of you. And then there's a lot of other legal stuff like what courts are going to be selling and all that kind of stuff. I'm not a lawyer. I'm not going to tell you about that. I'm just giving you the big picture of the things you have to talk about and that's things like the shopping cart access and the when you get paid and how the money flows and who the relaunches work. Also another big thing, one more big thing that goes along with that relaunch is the back end. Because a lot of times the launch sets up another sale for a higher end product down the road or maybe immediately after or maybe at a live event. Do you get paid on the back end sales or do you just get the upfront? And there's no right or wrong answer. Just know that that's another piece that can be and should be negotiated ahead of time.
